title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered to run events at a public library. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Clara, The Storytime Reader
Clara offered to read picture books on Saturday mornings after bringing her grandson to the library during a rainy school holiday. [47]She quickly learned that choosing a story was less important than judging when restless children needed to stand up, move or answer a question.[/47] Her experience as a nurse helped her stay calm when a toddler cried or a parent looked embarrassed. [48]She began placing cushions in a semicircle before families arrived, so latecomers could join without stepping over bags and feet.[/48] The first few sessions exhausted her voice, until a librarian showed her how to use pauses instead of volume. Clara says storytime is not performance in the theatrical sense; it is an invitation into shared attention. She still loves the moment when adults stop checking their phones because they want to know what happens to the dragon too, even though they pretend they are only listening for the children.
texts.B:
### Marco, The Homework Club Helper
Marco volunteered at the homework club because he had struggled with maths at school and remembered feeling stupid in crowded classrooms. [49]He was careful never to take the pencil from a pupil's hand, because he wanted them to own the solution.[/49] His work as an accountant helped him explain fractions through money, shopping, football scores and slices of imaginary pizza. The biggest challenge was helping several children at very different levels while parents waited nearby hoping for quick visible results before the library closed. [50]Marco felt especially pleased when a boy who always said “I can't” began correcting his own mistake before asking for help.[/50] [55]The club taught him that confidence often appears in small pauses rather than dramatic breakthroughs.[/55] He now keeps scrap paper, spare pencils, dice and coloured counters in his bag, since a change of object can unlock a stubborn problem without another lecture from an adult.
texts.C:
### Beth, The Local History Host
Beth ran monthly evenings where residents brought photographs, letters and memories of the neighbourhood, usually in biscuit tins. [51]Her main rule was that nobody could correct another person's memory until the speaker had finished telling the story.[/51] She had worked as a journalist, so she knew how to ask follow-up questions without making people feel interrogated. The sessions produced unexpected links: a shopkeeper recognised his childhood street in a stranger's holiday slide, and two cousins met for the first time. [52]Beth started recording permission forms more carefully after one visitor became upset that a family story might appear online.[/52] She says local history work requires tenderness as well as curiosity, especially when family pride is involved. Her favourite evenings end with people leaning over the same photograph, arguing gently about a doorway, a bus stop or the name of a long-closed bakery, while younger visitors discover that streets can hold secrets.
texts.D:
### Sam, The Digital Skills Coach
Sam helped older library users learn basic phone and computer skills on Wednesday afternoons in the reference room. [53]He discovered that the hardest lesson was not explaining passwords, but persuading people that making mistakes on a screen was not shameful.[/53] His patience came from teaching himself coding at night after factory shifts. He avoided jargon, repeated steps slowly, and asked learners to bring real tasks, such as booking appointments or sending holiday photos. [54]One woman returned the following week simply to show him that she had joined a family video call unaided.[/54] Sam realised the sessions were about independence, not gadgets, and that success could look very ordinary. The practical difficulty was maintaining privacy in a public room where people worried about bank details, medical messages and mistakes other users might see. [56]He now carries cardboard privacy screens and reminds every volunteer that a whispered question may have taken months of courage.[/56]

questions.question: learned to adjust an activity according to children's physical restlessness?
questions.explanation: Clara judged when children needed to stand, move or answer.

questions.question: arranged seating in advance so people could enter without disturbing others?
questions.explanation: Clara placed cushions in a semicircle before families arrived.

questions.question: deliberately avoided taking control of a learner's written work?
questions.explanation: Marco never took the pencil from a pupil's hand.

questions.question: was pleased when a learner began spotting their own error?
questions.explanation: Marco saw a boy correct his own mistake before asking for help.

questions.question: set a rule to prevent people interrupting or correcting a speaker too soon?
questions.explanation: Beth made sure no one corrected a memory before the speaker had finished.

questions.question: changed the way consent was recorded after someone worried about online sharing?
questions.explanation: Beth recorded permission forms more carefully after a visitor's concern.

questions.question: found that overcoming embarrassment was harder than teaching the technical content?
questions.explanation: Sam found shame about mistakes harder to address than passwords.

questions.question: mentions someone returning to demonstrate a new digital achievement?
questions.explanation: A woman returned to show Sam she had joined a video call alone.

questions.question: says quieter signs can reveal growing confidence better than dramatic progress?
questions.explanation: Marco says confidence often appears in small pauses.

questions.question: uses physical screens to protect people's privacy during help sessions?
questions.explanation: Sam carries cardboard privacy screens for learners.
